summary:
The Azilian culture was a post-Paleolithic hunter-gatherer tradition of southwestern Europe — particularly the Franco-Cantabrian region of southern France and northern Spain — dated to approximately 12,000–9,500 BCE, making it one of the earliest Mesolithic cultures in western Europe. It succeeded the Magdalenian culture and is characterized by a marked simplification of material culture: smaller, more geometric stone tools (microliths), the use of flat river pebbles painted with abstract geometric designs (pebbles aziliens), and the near-total disappearance of the elaborate figurative cave art and carved portable art that had characterized the Magdalenian.

The Azilian transition reflects the profound ecological and demographic changes accompanying the [[event:1262|end of the Ice Age]] in western Europe. As the great herds of cold-adapted game animals — reindeer, mammoth, wild horse — declined or migrated northward, Azilian communities adapted to the forests and diverse prey species of the postglacial landscape, developing the microlithic composite tool technology characteristic of European Mesolithic cultures. The painted pebbles, whose meaning remains unknown, represent one of the only surviving forms of Azilian symbolic expression, a dramatic contrast to the cave art grandeur of their Magdalenian predecessors. The Azilian forms part of the broader mosaic of western European Mesolithic cultures that persisted until the arrival of farming communities from Anatolia around 7000–5500 BCE.
